checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-006: Is BlobSidecarsByRoot deprecated protocol fully disabled after FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS?

reachability.notes: Similar to PROP-0047 but for the by-root variant. Consistent deprecation behavior across both variants is critical.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the BlobSidecarsByRoot request handler in the P2P layer. 2. Verify epoch calculation logic: confirm current_epoch >= FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS triggers full protocol disablement. 3. During transition period (FULU_FORK_EPOCH <= current_epoch < FULU_FORK_EPOCH + MIN_EPOCHS_FOR_BLOB_SIDECARS_REQUESTS): verify only pre-Fulu blobs are served when requested by root. 4. After transition: send BlobSidecarsByRoot requests and verify the protocol returns appropriate error/rejection (not data). 5. Compare deprecation behavior with BlobSidecarsByRange to ensure consistency between both variants. 6. Test edge cases at exact epoch boundaries. 7. Verify no resource exhaustion possible via deprecated protocol requests.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-54, Trust Boundary: tb-006, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-013:INV-001. Post-transition requests are minor resource waste. Inconsistent deprecation between by-range and by-root variants could confuse implementations.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does beacon-node correctly identify pre-Fulu vs post-Fulu blobs by root during transition period?

reachability.notes: Similar to PROP-0047 but for the by-root variant. Consistent deprecation behavior across both variants is critical.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify how blob root lookups determine the associated epoch/slot. 2. Verify that roots for pre-Fulu blobs are correctly identified and served. 3. Verify that roots for post-Fulu blobs are rejected during transition. 4. Test with malformed roots to ensure proper validation. 5. Verify no information leakage about blob existence through error responses. 6. Check that actor-req-resp-client and actor-beacon-node coordinate correctly on deprecation state.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-54, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001/tb-002: Is max_blobs_per_block from get_blob_parameters(epoch) consistent across all client implementations?

reachability.notes: Cross-client inconsistency in epoch-dependent blob parameters is the primary consensus split vector for EIP-7892. An attacker can craft blocks at parameter-change epochs to exploit any divergence.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify all implementations of get_blob_parameters() across client codebases (Lighthouse, Prysm, Teku, Lodestar, Nimbus, etc.). 2. For each parameter-change epoch boundary defined in EIP-7892, compute max_blobs_per_block across all clients. 3. Verify identical values returned for: (a) epochs before FULU_FORK_EPOCH, (b) FULU_FORK_EPOCH exactly, (c) epochs after FULU_FORK_EPOCH. 4. Test edge cases: epoch 0, epoch at fork boundary minus 1, fork boundary, fork boundary plus 1. 5. Craft test blocks with blob counts at max_blobs_per_block boundary and verify accept/reject consensus across clients. 6. Verify fork-choice implications: does a disagreement cause permanent chain split? 7. Check for off-by-one errors in epoch boundary calculations. 8. Verify any caching of blob parameters does not cause stale values across epoch transitions.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-55, Trust Boundaries: tb-001, tb-002, Source Assumption: asm-005, Source Threat: stride-008. Primary Element: p2p:SG-007:beacon_block_kzg_validation. PoC approach: identify epoch where clients compute different max_blobs_per_block and demonstrate resulting consensus split.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does beacon-node correctly enforce max_blobs_per_block limit during block/sidecar gossip validation?

reachability.notes: Cross-client inconsistency in epoch-dependent blob parameters is the primary consensus split vector for EIP-7892. An attacker can craft blocks at parameter-change epochs to exploit any divergence.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate beacon_block_kzg_validation logic (primary element: p2p:SG-007). 2. Trace how max_blobs_per_block is fetched during validation - verify it uses get_blob_parameters(block.epoch). 3. Submit blocks via P2P gossip with exactly max_blobs_per_block blobs - verify acceptance. 4. Submit blocks with max_blobs_per_block + 1 blobs - verify rejection. 5. Test at epoch boundaries where max_blobs_per_block changes value. 6. Verify rejection occurs before expensive KZG operations (DoS prevention). 7. Check that rejection produces consistent error codes across clients. 8. Verify sidecar validation uses same epoch-based parameter lookup as block validation.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-55, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node. Validates that internal enforcement logic correctly uses the cross-client consistent parameters.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-010/tb-011: Is proposer lookahead (EIP-7917) deterministic and consistent across all client implementations?

reachability.notes: An attacker publishes a sidecar with a proposer_index that is correct according to one client's shuffling but incorrect according to another's. This exploits cross-client proposer assignment divergence.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify all implementations of compute_proposer_for_slot() across client codebases (Lighthouse, Prysm, Teku, Lodestar, Nimbus, etc.). 2. For identical beacon state S, compute proposer assignments for all slots in the lookahead window across each client. 3. Verify identical proposer_index values returned for every slot in the lookahead window. 4. Test edge cases: (a) first slot of epoch, (b) last slot of epoch, (c) epoch boundary transitions, (d) fork epoch boundaries (FULU_FORK_EPOCH). 5. Verify shuffling algorithm implementation consistency: same RANDAO mix, same active validator set, same epoch. 6. Test with varying validator set sizes to catch integer overflow/modulo edge cases. 7. Check for off-by-one errors in slot/epoch calculations within lookahead window. 8. Verify effective_balance weighting is applied identically across clients. 9. Test proposer computation caching: ensure stale cache does not cause divergence on state transitions. 10. Craft test sidecar with proposer_index valid for one client but not another and observe accept/reject behavior.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-56, Trust Boundaries: tb-010, tb-011, Source Assumption: asm-009, Source Threat: stride-022. Primary Element: fork:SG-002:upgrade_to_fulu. PoC approach: demonstrate two clients computing different proposers for the same slot from the same state - this causes consensus split at the first divergent slot.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does gossip validation correctly reject sidecars with incorrect proposer_index?

reachability.notes: An attacker publishes a sidecar with a proposer_index that is correct according to one client's shuffling but incorrect according to another's. This exploits cross-client proposer assignment divergence.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate gossip validation logic for beacon blocks and data sidecars. 2. Trace how proposer_index is validated - verify it calls compute_proposer_for_slot(state, sidecar.slot). 3. Submit sidecar via P2P gossip with correct proposer_index for the slot - verify acceptance. 4. Submit sidecar with incorrect proposer_index (e.g., off by 1) - verify rejection with appropriate error. 5. Test at epoch boundaries where proposer shuffling changes. 6. Verify rejection occurs early in validation pipeline (before expensive signature verification). 7. Check that error responses do not leak information about expected proposer_index. 8. Verify beacon-node and validator-set actors coordinate correctly on proposer assignment.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-56, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node, actor-validator-set, actor-proposer. Validates that internal enforcement of proposer checks uses the cross-client consistent lookahead computation.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the proposer lookahead window correctly handle fork transitions at FULU_FORK_EPOCH?

reachability.notes: An attacker publishes a sidecar with a proposer_index that is correct according to one client's shuffling but incorrect according to another's. This exploits cross-client proposer assignment divergence.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify how upgrade_to_fulu (primary element: fork:SG-002) affects proposer computation. 2. Verify proposer lookahead window calculations across the FULU_FORK_EPOCH boundary. 3. Test slots in lookahead window that span pre-Fulu and post-Fulu epochs. 4. Verify any new proposer selection rules introduced by EIP-7917 are applied consistently. 5. Check that fork version is correctly incorporated into any domain separations affecting proposer computation. 6. Test reorg scenarios where chain tip crosses fork boundary - verify proposer assignments recalculate correctly. 7. Verify no divergence between clients when lookahead window extends into post-fork epochs from pre-fork state.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-56, Primary Element: fork:SG-002:upgrade_to_fulu. Fork transitions are high-risk for cross-client divergence due to potential differences in when new rules apply.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Is KZG commitment verification cryptographically sound against forged data column sidecars?

reachability.notes: This is the fundamental cryptographic soundness assumption. If KZG verification accepts invalid data, the entire data availability scheme is broken. Implementation bugs in the KZG library are the primary attack vector.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify all entry points where verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch is called during sidecar validation. 2. Review the KZG library implementation for: (a) correct pairing operations, (b) proper point validation (subgroup checks), (c) correct domain separation. 3. Attempt to forge a data column sidecar where S.column data is inconsistent with S.kzg_commitments but passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch. 4. Test with: (a) zero proofs, (b) identity elements, (c) points not on curve, (d) points in wrong subgroup. 5. Verify batch verification does not introduce vulnerabilities not present in individual verification. 6. Test edge cases: empty column, maximum column size, single cell. 7. Verify commitment binding: given commitment C, it should be computationally infeasible to find two different data D1 != D2 that both verify. 8. Check for timing side channels that could leak information about valid vs invalid proofs. 9. Verify KZG trusted setup is correctly loaded and validated on node startup.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-57, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Source Assumption: asm-004, Source Threat: stride-002. Primary Element: p2p:SG-003:verify_data_column_sidecar_kzg_proofs. PoC approach: forge a data column sidecar with incorrect data that passes verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch - this would demonstrate complete DA scheme compromise.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Is inclusion proof verification correctly integrated with KZG commitment verification?

reachability.notes: This is the fundamental cryptographic soundness assumption. If KZG verification accepts invalid data, the entire data availability scheme is broken. Implementation bugs in the KZG library are the primary attack vector.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify how inclusion proofs link data column sidecars to beacon block roots. 2. Verify the Merkle path from sidecar commitment to block body root is validated. 3. Attempt to craft a sidecar with valid KZG proofs but invalid inclusion proof - verify rejection. 4. Attempt to craft a sidecar with valid inclusion proof but for a different commitment than the one in the sidecar. 5. Test inclusion proof verification at tree boundaries and edge cases. 6. Verify that both KZG verification AND inclusion proof verification must pass for sidecar acceptance. 7. Check ordering of verification steps - ensure inclusion proof check cannot be bypassed. 8. Verify actor-p2p-peer and actor-beacon-node correctly coordinate on both verification types.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-57, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. Both KZG soundness AND correct inclusion proof binding are required for DA security.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does KZG proof batch verification maintain soundness properties of individual verification?

reachability.notes: This is the fundamental cryptographic soundness assumption. If KZG verification accepts invalid data, the entire data availability scheme is broken. Implementation bugs in the KZG library are the primary attack vector.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Review verify_cell_kzg_proof_batch implementation for random linear combination technique. 2. Verify randomness source for batch verification is cryptographically secure. 3. Test batch of N proofs where 1 is invalid - verify batch fails. 4. Test batch of N proofs where N-1 are invalid - verify batch fails. 5. Test empty batch handling - verify defined behavior (should fail or be rejected earlier). 6. Test maximum batch size - verify no integer overflow in aggregation. 7. Verify batch verification result matches individual verification for all items in batch. 8. Check for potential rogue key attacks if proofs from multiple sources are batched. 9. Verify batch verification does not introduce false positive rate (accepting invalid proofs with non-negligible probability).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-57. Batch verification is an optimization that must not weaken security. A flaw here could allow selective invalid data injection.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Are structural validation checks performed BEFORE expensive KZG/inclusion proof verification in the gossip validation pipeline?

reachability.notes: If expensive KZG verification is performed before cheap structural checks, an attacker can trigger O(n) expensive operations with O(1) effort by sending structurally invalid sidecars.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the data_column_sidecar gossip validation function (primary element: p2p:SG-006). 2. Trace the validation order and verify the following sequence: (a) Index bounds check (column index < NUMBER_OF_COLUMNS), (b) Empty data check (sidecar.column is not empty), (c) max_blobs check (len(sidecar.kzg_commitments) <= max_blobs_per_block), (d) Length consistency check (len(column) == len(kzg_commitments) == len(kzg_proofs)), (e) THEN proposer signature verification, (f) THEN KZG proof verification, (g) THEN inclusion proof verification. 3. Craft malformed sidecars that fail each structural check and verify: (a) They are rejected immediately without triggering expensive operations, (b) Measure CPU time to confirm cheap rejection. 4. Test attack scenario: send burst of structurally invalid sidecars and measure node CPU load - should remain low. 5. Verify proposer signature check occurs before KZG verification to act as cost gate (signature verification is cheaper than KZG). 6. Check for any code paths that bypass the ordering (e.g., caching, early returns).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-58, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Source Threat: stride-003. Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:data_column_sidecar_gossip_validation. PoC approach: send structurally invalid sidecars and demonstrate disproportionate CPU cost if ordering is incorrect.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does proposer signature verification act as effective cost gate before KZG verification?

reachability.notes: If expensive KZG verification is performed before cheap structural checks, an attacker can trigger O(n) expensive operations with O(1) effort by sending structurally invalid sidecars.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Verify proposer signature check is performed after structural checks but before KZG verification. 2. Confirm that signature verification has significantly lower cost than KZG verification (benchmark both operations). 3. Test sidecars with valid structure but invalid proposer signature - verify rejection before KZG. 4. Measure the cost amplification factor: (cost of signature check) / (cost of KZG verification). 5. Verify that the proposer must be a valid validator index before signature verification (prevents invalid key lookup). 6. Check that signature verification uses correct domain separation for the fork epoch. 7. Verify no caching mechanisms allow bypassing signature check on repeated submissions.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-58, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. The signature check serves as a cost barrier - only sidecars from valid proposers trigger expensive KZG verification.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Are all structural validation checks O(1) complexity relative to sidecar size?

reachability.notes: If expensive KZG verification is performed before cheap structural checks, an attacker can trigger O(n) expensive operations with O(1) effort by sending structurally invalid sidecars.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Review each structural check for time complexity: (a) Index bounds check - verify O(1) comparison, (b) Empty check - verify O(1) length check, (c) max_blobs check - verify O(1) length comparison, (d) Length consistency - verify O(1) length comparisons (not iterating through data). 2. Verify no hidden O(n) operations in structural checks (e.g., deserialization of entire column data before length check). 3. Benchmark structural checks with maximum-size sidecars - verify constant time regardless of data size. 4. Check that SSZ deserialization does not fully parse column data before structural validation. 5. Verify memory allocation during structural checks is bounded and does not depend on attacker-controlled sizes. 6. Test with oversized fields (exceeding max values) to ensure bounds checks prevent allocation attacks.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-58. Structural checks must be truly cheap - O(n) structural checks defeat the purpose of ordering them first.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-003: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRange serve complete block responses (all columns or none) for columns in storage?

reachability.notes: Partial block responses could cause syncing nodes to conclude that some columns are unavailable when they are actually available, failing data availability checks.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRange request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-008:data_column_sidecars_by_range). 2. Identify the response construction logic that iterates over blocks and columns in the requested range. 3. For a block B with columns C1, C2, ..., Cn in storage, verify that the response either includes ALL columns for B or NONE. 4. Test scenario: request columns for a block range where server has partial column storage (e.g., columns 0-63 but not 64-127). Verify either all stored columns are returned or none are returned for that block. 5. Test with various storage states: (a) all columns available, (b) some columns available, (c) no columns available. 6. Verify no race conditions between storage reads that could cause partial responses (e.g., column pruned between iteration steps). 7. Test concurrent requests to ensure atomicity of response construction. 8. Verify response ordering is consistent and predictable. 9. Check error handling: if partial storage is detected mid-response, verify clean termination without partial block data.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-59, Trust Boundary: tb-003, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-008:INV-004. PoC approach: demonstrate a server returning partial columns for a block, causing the requester to fail data availability when columns were actually available.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the req-resp client correctly interpret partial block responses as unavailability?

reachability.notes: Partial block responses could cause syncing nodes to conclude that some columns are unavailable when they are actually available, failing data availability checks.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the client-side response parsing logic for DataColumnSidecarsByRange in actor-req-resp-client. 2. Verify that when a peer returns partial columns for a block, the client either: (a) treats the entire block as unavailable from that peer and retries with another peer, or (b) requests the missing columns specifically. 3. Test with malicious peer returning intentionally partial responses - verify the client does not conclude data is unavailable without exhausting other peers. 4. Verify timeout and retry logic handles partial responses correctly. 5. Check that partial responses do not poison caches or data structures. 6. Verify metrics/logging for partial response detection to aid debugging. 7. Test integration with data availability sampling (DAS) logic to ensure partial responses do not cause false negatives.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-59, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. The client must be resilient to malicious or buggy peers returning partial data.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Is there atomicity between storage check and response generation in DataColumnSidecarsByRange?

reachability.notes: Partial block responses could cause syncing nodes to conclude that some columns are unavailable when they are actually available, failing data availability checks.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the storage layer interface used by DataColumnSidecarsByRange handler. 2. Check if there is a single atomic query to determine which columns are available for a block, or if columns are checked/fetched individually. 3. If individual checks: verify locking or snapshot isolation prevents interleaved pruning/deletion. 4. Test race condition: initiate DataColumnSidecarsByRange request while concurrently triggering column pruning. Verify no partial block responses occur. 5. Review database/storage transaction boundaries - ensure response construction happens within a consistent snapshot. 6. Verify that 'columns in storage' check and 'column fetch' are atomic or use consistent view. 7. Check for TOCTOU (time-of-check-time-of-use) vulnerabilities between availability check and data fetch. 8. Verify beacon-node correctly coordinates storage access to prevent partial responses.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-59, Covered Nodes: actor-beacon-node. Storage layer race conditions are a common source of partial/inconsistent responses.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-004: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRoot respond with at least one sidecar when matching data is available?

reachability.notes: If a server silently drops available sidecars from its response, requesting nodes would conclude the data is unavailable, failing data availability checks.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-009:data_column_sidecars_by_root). 2. Identify the response construction logic that retrieves sidecars by their root identifiers. 3. For a request containing root R where the server has a matching sidecar S in storage, verify that S is included in the response. 4. Test with single root request: request a root known to exist - verify non-empty response. 5. Test with multiple roots: request N roots where M are available - verify exactly M sidecars returned. 6. Verify no silent filtering or dropping of valid, available sidecars occurs in the response path. 7. Test edge cases: (a) request root that exists but sidecar was recently pruned, (b) request during concurrent storage operations. 8. Verify logging/metrics exist to detect silent drops for debugging purposes. 9. Check that response serialization does not truncate or drop items due to size limits without proper error signaling. 10. Test with maximum request size to ensure all matching sidecars are returned.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-60, Trust Boundary: tb-004, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-009:INV-003. PoC approach: demonstrate a server that has sidecars but returns empty response, causing the requester to incorrectly conclude data unavailability.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the req-resp client correctly handle empty responses from DataColumnSidecarsByRoot?

reachability.notes: If a server silently drops available sidecars from its response, requesting nodes would conclude the data is unavailable, failing data availability checks.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the client-side response parsing logic for DataColumnSidecarsByRoot in actor-req-resp-client. 2. Verify that when a peer returns an empty response for roots that should exist, the client retries with other peers before concluding unavailability. 3. Test with malicious peer returning empty response for valid roots - verify the client does not immediately fail DA checks. 4. Verify peer scoring: does returning empty responses for available data result in peer reputation penalty? 5. Check retry logic: how many peers are queried before concluding data is truly unavailable? 6. Verify timeout handling: distinguish between 'peer has no data' vs 'peer did not respond'. 7. Test integration with data availability sampling (DAS) to ensure empty responses from one peer do not cause false unavailability conclusions. 8. Verify beacon-node and req-resp-client coordinate correctly on retry and fallback logic.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-60, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. Client must be resilient to malicious peers returning empty responses for available data.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Is there any filtering or rate-limiting logic in DataColumnSidecarsByRoot that could silently drop valid sidecars?

reachability.notes: If a server silently drops available sidecars from its response, requesting nodes would conclude the data is unavailable, failing data availability checks.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Review the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot handler for any response size limits or rate limiting that could cause partial responses. 2. Check if there are maximum response size constraints that could truncate results - if so, verify proper error signaling. 3. Verify no internal caching or filtering logic drops sidecars based on non-existence criteria. 4. Check for any priority ordering that could cause lower-priority sidecars to be dropped. 5. Review error handling paths: verify no exceptions cause partial response without error indication. 6. Test under high load: verify rate limiting does not cause silent sidecar drops. 7. Verify any request throttling returns appropriate error codes rather than silently truncating responses. 8. Check for off-by-one errors in iteration logic that could skip the last sidecar in a batch.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-60. Implementation details like size limits or rate limiting could inadvertently violate the 'respond with at least one if available' invariant.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-004: Does DataColumnSidecarsByRoot refuse to serve sidecars for blocks that fail beacon-chain state transition?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes for blocks that failed state transition. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle and distributes invalid data.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot request handler (primary element: p2p:SG-009:data_column_sidecars_by_root). 2. Identify the validation logic that checks whether a block passed beacon-chain state transition before serving its data columns. 3. Store block data for a block that fails state transition validation (e.g., invalid signature, invalid state root, double vote). 4. Request data column sidecars by root for this invalid block. 5. Verify the server returns an empty response or appropriate error, NOT the stored data columns. 6. Test various state transition failure modes: (a) invalid proposer signature, (b) invalid state root, (c) invalid parent root, (d) blocks from orphaned/pruned forks. 7. Verify the check occurs BEFORE data is serialized and sent. 8. Test race condition: store block data, then mark block as invalid - verify no window where invalid data can be served. 9. Check for caching that could serve stale positive results for blocks later marked invalid. 10. Verify logging captures attempts to request data for invalid blocks (security monitoring).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-61, Trust Boundary: tb-004, Source Invariant: p2p:SG-009:INV-005, Source Threat: stride-013. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle revealing which blocks failed state transition and distributes invalid data to the network.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Is block state transition validity correctly tracked and accessible to the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot handler?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes for blocks that failed state transition. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle and distributes invalid data.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify how block state transition validity is stored and tracked in the beacon node. 2. Verify the DataColumnSidecarsByRoot handler queries this validity status before serving data. 3. Check for consistency between block storage and validity tracking - no orphaned data without validity status. 4. Test scenario: block data is stored before state transition completes - verify data is not served until validation passes. 5. Verify actors actor-req-resp-client and actor-beacon-node correctly coordinate on block validity status. 6. Check that validity status is not just 'in canonical chain' but specifically 'passed state transition'. 7. Verify handling of blocks that initially passed validation but were later reorged out. 8. Test edge case: request for block root that exists in storage but has never been validated (e.g., from untrusted import).
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-61, Covered Nodes: actor-req-resp-client, actor-beacon-node. The validity check depends on accurate tracking of which blocks passed state transition.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Are there any bypass paths that could serve data columns for invalid blocks?

reachability.notes: An attacker probes for blocks that failed state transition. Serving data for invalid blocks acts as an oracle and distributes invalid data.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Review all code paths leading to DataColumnSidecarsByRoot response generation. 2. Identify any early-return optimizations that might skip validity checks (e.g., 'block root in recent cache'). 3. Check if there are admin/debug endpoints that bypass validity checks. 4. Verify no separate 'raw data' API exists that serves data without validity checks. 5. Check for feature flags or configuration options that could disable validity checks. 6. Verify error handling paths: if validity check throws exception, verify data is not served. 7. Test with concurrent validity status updates: request data while block is being marked invalid. 8. Verify no information leakage through response timing (e.g., faster rejection for invalid blocks reveals validity status). 9. Check that migrated/imported historical data also respects validity checks.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-61. Implementation shortcuts or debug features could inadvertently bypass the validity check, serving as an oracle for block validity.

checklist_item.title: Verify Trust Boundary Integrity for tb-001: Does the gossip validation pipeline IGNORE (not REJECT) sidecars whose parent block has not yet been seen?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Locate the gossip validation handler for data column sidecars (primary element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_parent_seen). 2. Identify the validation step that checks if the parent block has been seen by the node. 3. Verify the validation result when parent is not seen: must return IGNORE, not REJECT. Trace the code path to confirm the return value. 4. Submit a sidecar via P2P gossip referencing a parent block that has not yet arrived - verify the sidecar is not rejected but queued/ignored. 5. Verify the sidecar is stored in a pending/orphan queue awaiting parent arrival. 6. Subsequently submit the parent block - verify the previously-ignored sidecar is re-validated and processed. 7. Test timing: verify sidecar arrives before parent, is ignored, then parent arrives, sidecar is re-validated successfully. 8. Verify IGNORE does not result in peer penalty (unlike REJECT which typically penalizes the sender). 9. Test with various parent block scenarios: (a) parent arrives within timeout, (b) parent never arrives, (c) parent arrives but is invalid.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62, Trust Boundary: tb-001, Source Threat: stride-026. Primary Element: p2p:SG-006:q_check_parent_seen. Incorrect REJECT behavior would cause valid sidecars to be permanently dropped, breaking data availability for out-of-order arrivals.

checklist_item.title: Critical Boundary Check: Is the IGNORE queue for unseen-parent sidecars bounded to prevent memory exhaustion?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the data structure used to queue sidecars awaiting parent block arrival. 2. Verify the queue has explicit size limits (maximum number of pending sidecars or maximum memory usage). 3. Determine the eviction policy when the queue is full: (a) drop oldest entries, (b) drop entries for least-seen parents, (c) reject new entries. 4. Calculate maximum memory consumption: max_queue_size * max_sidecar_size. Verify this is a reasonable bound (e.g., < 1GB). 5. Test attack scenario: flood the node with sidecars referencing unique unseen parent block roots - each requires a separate queue entry. 6. Measure memory consumption during attack - verify it is bounded and does not grow indefinitely. 7. Verify the queue bound is per-peer or global, and how it interacts with peer limits. 8. Test queue eviction: when a legitimate sidecar is evicted due to attack traffic, verify it can be re-requested or re-gossiped. 9. Verify metrics/alerts exist for queue saturation to detect ongoing attacks. 10. Check that queue operations are O(1) or O(log n) to prevent CPU exhaustion during queue management.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62, Trust Boundary: tb-001. PoC: flood node with sidecars referencing unique unseen parents, measuring memory consumption. Unbounded queue allows trivial memory exhaustion attack.

checklist_item.title: Supporting Check: Does the beacon-node correctly trigger re-validation of queued sidecars when the parent block arrives?

reachability.notes: An attacker floods sidecars referencing different unseen parents, each requiring queuing. The IGNORE queue must be bounded to prevent memory exhaustion.

checklist_item.test_procedure: 1. Identify the callback/hook mechanism that triggers re-validation when a new block is received. 2. Verify that when a block B arrives, all sidecars queued with parent_root == B.root are retrieved and re-validated. 3. Test the re-validation path: verify full gossip validation is applied (not a partial re-check). 4. Verify re-validation respects the same validation rules as initial validation. 5. Test ordering: if multiple sidecars reference the same parent, verify all are re-validated. 6. Test cascading: if sidecar S1 references parent B1, and B1 references parent B0 (also unseen), verify correct behavior when B0 then B1 arrive. 7. Verify no duplicate processing: if sidecar was already re-validated, prevent re-processing. 8. Test timeout: sidecars in queue too long without parent arrival should be dropped (configurable timeout). 9. Verify actor-p2p-peer and actor-beacon-node coordinate correctly on block arrival notifications and queue lookups. 10. Check for race conditions: block arrives while sidecar is being added to queue - verify no missed re-validation.
checklist_item.notes: Source: PROP-W4B13-62, Covered Nodes: actor-p2p-peer, actor-beacon-node. Failure to re-validate would cause valid sidecars to be permanently stuck in the queue.
